title: Training - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Introduction
Model training is a crucial phase in the development of machine learning models, involving the process of teaching a model to recognize patterns and make predictions based on input data. The key steps in model training are as follows:
1. Model Selection:
Choose an appropriate machine learning algorithm based on the nature of the problem (e.g., regression, classification) and the characteristics of the data.
Consider factors such as model complexity, interpretability, and the volume of data available.
2. Training the Model:
Feed the training data into the selected model.
The model learns to recognize patterns by adjusting its internal parameters during an iterative optimization process.
The objective is to minimize the difference between the model's predictions and the actual target values.

title: Install and Configure Tomcat 10 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Navigate to the /tmp directory for temporary file handling.
cd /tmp
sudo wget https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat/tomcat-10/v10.1.39/bin/apache-tomcat-10.1.39.tar.gz
Download Tomcat 10.1.39 archive from the official Apache archive.
sudo tar -xvf apache-tomcat-10.1.39.tar.gz
Extract the downloaded
.tar.gz
archive.
sudo mv apache-tomcat-10.1.39 /opt/tomcat
Move the extracted folder to
/opt
, renaming it to
tomcat
.
2. Set Ownership and Permissions
sudo chown -R inferyx:inferyx /opt/tomcat
Assign ownership of the Tomcat directory to user
inferyx
.
sudo chmod -R u+x /opt/tomcat
Ensure the user has execute permissions on all files.
3. Create Legacy Init Script (Optional)
sudo nano /etc/init.d/tomcat
Create and open a legacy init script.
#!/bin/sh
export CATALINA_HOME="/opt/tomcat"
export JAVA_HOME="/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk-amd64"
RETVAL=$?
case $1 in
start)
if [ -f $CATALINA_HOME/bin/startup.sh ]; then
echo $"Starting Tomcat"
su -p -s /bin/sh inferyx $CATALINA_HOME/bin/startup.sh
fi
;;
stop)
if [ -f $CATALINA_HOME/bin/shutdown.sh ]; then
echo $"Stopping Tomcat"
su -p -s /bin/sh inferyx $CATALINA_HOME/bin/shutdown.sh
fi
;;
*)
echo $"Usage: $0 {start|stop}"
exit 1
;;
esac
exit $RETVAL
4. Create systemd Service
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/tomcat.service
Open systemd service file for Tomcat.
[Unit]
Description=Apache Tomcat Web Application
After=network.target
[Service]
Type=forking
Environment=CATALINA_PID=/opt/tomcat/temp/tomcat.pid
Environment=CATALINA_HOME=/opt/tomcat
Environment=CATALINA_BASE=/opt/tomcat
ExecStart=/opt/tomcat/bin/startup.sh
ExecStop=/opt/tomcat/bin/shutdown.sh
User=inferyx
Group=inferyx
UMask=0007
RestartSec=10
Restart=always
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
5. Reload systemd and Enable Tomcat
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
Reload systemd to recognize the new service file.
sudo systemctl enable tomcat
Enable the Tomcat service to start on boot.
sudo systemctl start tomcat
Start the Tomcat service.
6. Create
setenv.sh
for Environment Configuration
cd /opt/tomcat/bin
Navigate to Tomcat's
bin
directory.
sudo nano setenv.sh
Create the
setenv.sh
file to configure environment variables.
#!/bin/bash
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk-amd64
export CATALINA_OPTS="-Xms4g -Xmx8g -Dapp.base.dir=/app/framework"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.lang=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.io=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.util=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.util.concurrent=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.rmi/sun.rmi.transport=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.time=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/sun.nio.ch=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.lang.invoke=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/java.nio=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS --add-opens=java.base/sun.util.calendar=ALL-UNNAMED"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -agentlib:jdwp=transport=dt_socket,server=y,suspend=n,address=*:8000"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -Dframework.property=/app/framework/conf/framework.properties"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -Dlogback.configurationFile=/opt/tomcat/conf/log4j2.xml"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -Dframework.log.path=/app/framework/logs"
export CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -Dframework.config=/app/framework/conf/framework.properties"
export CLASSPATH=/opt/tomcat/bin/bootstrap.jar:/opt/tomcat/bin/tomcat-juli.jar
CATALINA_OPTS="$CATALINA_OPTS -Djava.class.path.print=true"
echo "CLASSPATH: $CLASSPATH"
sudo chmod +x setenv.sh
Make
setenv.sh
executable.
7. Verify Tomcat
sudo systemctl status tomcat
Check if Tomcat is running properly.
tail -f /opt/tomcat/logs/catalina.out
View live logs for troubleshooting.

Request the Download File
Choose one of the below options to download the patch file.
Option 1: Using
curl
To obtain the necessary download file for the patch, use the following
curl
command. This will authenticate and download the required tar file directly to your system.
curl https://dev.inferyx.com/download/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz
File Name:
inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz
OR
Option 2: Using
scp
Alternatively, you can securely copy (scp) the tar file from the remote server. Replace
<username>
,
<remote_host>
, and
/path/to/
with the appropriate values.
scp <username>@<remote_host>:/path/to/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz .
Note:
The dot (
.
) at the end of the command saves the file to your current working directory.
Navigate to the Directory
First, navigate to the
/install/framework/build/
directory:
cd /install/framework/build/
Extract the tar file
into the
/install/framework/build/
directory:
tar -xvf /install/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz
File Name -
inferyx-platform-test-v5.4.4.5
Navigate to the Deployment Directory
cd /install/framework/build/inferyx-platform-test-v5.4.4.5/deploy/bin
Run the
upgrade_framework_7.sh
Script
./upgrade_framework_7.sh -h

-u <username>
: MongoDB username
-p <******>
: MongoDB password
-b <backup>
: Skip backup if set to
no
-r <redeploy>
: Repatch if set to
yes
-D <database>
: Repoint MongoDB database
-m <username>
: MySQL username
-n <******>
: MySQL password
-h, --help
: Show help message
Example command to run the script:
./upgrade_framework_7.sh -u inferyx -p 20Inferyx19 -b no -r no
The -r option is not mandatory. Use this option only if you need to patch the same version again.
Access the Platform
Open web browser and verify that the application is accessible and you can log in as a user:
URL
:
http://localhost:8080/framework/app/index.html
or
http://<server_ip>/framework/app/index.html

title: Datapod - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Datapod serves as a database table reference within the Inferyx Platform, with its associated metadata stored in MongoDB. This comprehensive integration allows users to not only create intricate views within the database but also seamlessly register these complex views as datapods on the Inferyx Platform. By enabling users to harness the flexibility of creating and registering complex views, Inferyx facilitates a sophisticated approach to data modeling and analysis. This integration underscores the platform's commitment to accommodating diverse data structures and empowering users to derive valuable insights from their data with efficiency and precision.

title: Analytics Workbench - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence

Introduction
The Analytics Workbench provides a powerful and intuitive platform for data analysis and exploration, designed to meet the needs of data professionals and analysts. It encompasses three core modules: SQL Manager, File Upload, and Notebook, each tailored to streamline analytical workflows. The SQL Manager module offers robust capabilities for querying and managing data across various databases, enabling users to execute complex SQL operations with ease. The File Upload module facilitates the seamless import of data in diverse formats—such as CSVs, spreadsheets, and other files—into the workbench for processing and analysis. The Notebook module delivers an interactive environment where users can write and execute code,document their analytical processes, enhancing the platform’s flexibility and depth. Together, these modules can be leveraged independently or orchestrated into a unified analytics pipeline, providing precision and adaptability for a wide range of data-driven tasks.

title: Install and Configure Kafka-Confluent 7.8.0 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
1. Download and Install Confluent Platform
Step 1: Download the Confluent Platform Tarball
curl -O https://packages.confluent.io/archive/7.8/confluent-community-7.8.0.tar.gz
Step 2: Extract the Tarball
tar -xvf confluent-community-7.8.0.tar.gz
Step 3: Move the Extracted Files to
/opt/
sudo mv confluent-7.8.0/ /opt/
Step 4: Change Directory to Confluent Installation Folder
cd /opt/confluent-7.8.0/
Step 5: Verify the Confluent Platform Files
ls /opt/confluent-7.8.0
2. Set Environment Variables
Step 1: Set the
CONFLUENT_HOME
Environment Variable
export CONFLUENT_HOME=/opt/confluent-7.8.0
Step 2: Add Confluent Platform Binaries to the
PATH
export PATH=$PATH:$CONFLUENT_HOME/bin
Step 3: Verify the Environment Variables
echo $CONFLUENT_HOME
echo $PATH
3. Install and Configure Confluent Hub Client
Step 1: Create a Temporary Directory
mkdir -p /tmp/kafka
Step 2: Download the Confluent Hub Client
wget http://client.hub.confluent.io/confluent-hub-client-latest.tar.gz
Step 3: Extract the Confluent Hub Client
tar -xvf confluent-hub-client-latest.tar.gz
Step 4: Copy Files to the Confluent Platform Directory
cp -R bin/* /opt/confluent-7.8.0/bin/
cp -R etc/* /opt/confluent-7.8.0/etc/
cp -R share/java/* /opt/confluent-7.8.0/share/java/
4. Install Kafka Connector for MongoDB
Step 1: Install the MongoDB Kafka Connector
Use the Confluent Hub Client to install the MongoDB Kafka Connector version 1.10.0:
confluent-hub install mongodb/kafka-connect-mongodb:1.10.0
5. Install Confluent CLI
Step 1: Create the APT Keyrings Directory
sudo mkdir -p /etc/apt/keyrings
Step 2: Download and Add the Confluent CLI GPG Key
curl https://packages.confluent.io/confluent-cli/deb/archive.key | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/confluent-cli.gpg
Step 3: Set Permissions on the GPG Key
sudo chmod go+r /etc/apt/keyrings/confluent-cli.gpg
Step 4: Add the Confluent CLI Repository to APT Sources
echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/confluent-cli.gpg] https://packages.confluent.io/confluent-cli/deb stable main" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/confluent-cli.list >/dev/null
Step 5: Update the APT Package List
sudo apt update
Step 6: Install Confluent CLI
sudo apt install confluent-cli
6. Verify Confluent Platform Installation
Step 1: Check Confluent CLI Version
confluent version
Step 2: Verify Kafka Broker and Zookeeper
confluent local services status
7. Start and Stop Confluent Services
Step 1: Start Services
confluent local services start
Step 2: Check Services Status
confluent local services status
Step 3: Stop Services
confluent local services stop

title: Install and Configure Kafka-Confluent 6.1.1 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
This document outlines the steps to install and configure Kafka offline using Confluent Platform. The example provided uses version 6.1.1 of the Confluent Platform.
Prerequisites
Linux-based system
Internet access to download the Confluent Platform package (optional if you have the package locally)
Steps to Install Confluent Platform Offline
Step 1: Download and Extract Confluent Platform
Navigate to the desired directory, e.g.,
/app
.
cd /install/
Download the Confluent Platform tarball. If you have internet access:
sudo wget http://packages.confluent.io/archive/6.1/confluent-6.1.1.tar.gz
If you don't have internet access, ensure you have the
confluent-6.1.1.tar.gz
file available locally and skip to the next step.
Extract the tarball.
sudo tar -xvf confluent-6.1.1.tar.gz
Move the extracted directory to
/opt
.
sudo mv confluent-6.1.1 /opt
Step 2: Set Environment Variables
Set the
CONFLUENT_HOME
environment variable to the Confluent Platform directory.
export CONFLUENT_HOME=/opt/confluent-6.1.1
Add the Confluent Platform
bin
directory to your
PATH
.
export PATH=$PATH:$CONFLUENT_HOME/bin
(Optional) To make these changes permanent, add the above lines to your shell profile (e.g.,
.bashrc
or
.profile
).
echo 'export CONFLUENT_HOME=/opt/confluent-6.1.1' >> ~/.bashrc echo 'export PATH=$PATH:$CONFLUENT_HOME/bin' >> ~/.bashrc source ~/.bashrc
Step 3: Start Confluent Services
Start the Confluent services locally.
confluent local services start
Step 4: Install Kafka Connect Plugins(optional)
Navigate to the desired directory, e.g.,
/opt/confluent-6.1.1/bin/
cd /opt/confluent-6.1.1/bin/
Install the Kafka Connect JDBC plugin.
./confluent-hub install confluentinc/kafka-connect-jdbc:latest
Install the Kafka Connect Neo4j plugin.
./confluent-hub install neo4j/kafka-connect-neo4j:2.0.1
Step 5: Access Confluent Control Center
Open your browser and navigate to
http://your_ip_addres:9021
to access the Confluent Control Center.
Summary
This document provides the necessary steps to install Confluent Platform offline and configure the environment to start using Kafka and its associated services. Follow the steps carefully to ensure a smooth installation and setup. If you encounter any issues, refer to the Confluent Platform documentation or community forums for assistance.

title: Install and Configure Java 8 ,11,17,21 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Installing the  JRE/JDK
The easiest option for installing Java is to use the version packaged with Ubuntu.
To install this version, first update the package index:
sudo apt update
You can install Java 8 on Ubuntu 22.04/20.04/18.04/16.04 from OpenJDK binaries available on the default upstream repositories. Run the command:
sudo apt install openjdk-8-jdk
Verify the installation with:
java -version
You’ll see the following output:
openjdk version "1.8.0_312"
OpenJDK Runtime Environment (build 1.8.0_312-8u312-b07-0ubuntu1~20.04-b07)
OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM (build 25.312-b07, mixed mode)
Install Java 11 and Java 17
Update Package Index
Before installing Java, update the package index:
sudo apt update
Since you already have Java 8, install Java 11 and 17 using:
sudo apt install openjdk-11-jdk -y
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y
sudo apt install openjdk-21-jdk -y
Verify Installed Java Versions
update-alternatives --list java
You should see paths similar to:
/usr/lib/jvm/java-8-openjdk-amd64/jre/bin/java
/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64/bin/java
/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64/bin/java
/usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk-amd64/bin/java
Switch Between Java Versions
sudo update-alternatives --config java
You'll see an output like this:
There are 3 choices for the alternative java (providing /usr/bin/java).
Selection    Path                                            Priority   Status
------------------------------------------------------------
0            /usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64/bin/java     1711      auto mode
1            /usr/lib/jvm/java-8-openjdk-amd64/bin/java      1081      manual mode
2            /usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64/bin/java     1111      manual mode
3            /usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64/bin/java     1711      manual mode
4            /usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk-amd64/bin/java     2111      manual mode
Press <enter> to keep the current choice[*], or type selection number:
Enter the selection number for the Java version you want to use.

title: Model - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Machine learning models are computational algorithms designed to enable systems to learn patterns, make predictions, and perform tasks without being explicitly programmed. These models leverage statistical techniques to analyze and interpret data, uncovering intricate patterns and relationships. In the context of data science, machine learning models are utilized for various purposes, including classification, regression, clustering, and recommendation.
To deploy a machine learning model:
Training Phase:
The model is exposed to a labeled dataset, allowing it to learn and adapt to the underlying patterns within the data. During this phase, the model adjusts its parameters to optimize performance.
Testing and Validation:
The trained model is evaluated using a separate dataset to assess its accuracy and generalizability. This step ensures that the model can make reliable predictions on new, unseen data.
Deployment:
Once validated, the model is deployed for real-world use. It takes input data, processes it using the learned patterns, and produces predictions or classifications, contributing valuable insights to decision-making processes.
Machine learning models can range from traditional algorithms like linear regression to more complex ones such as neural networks in deep learning. The choice of the model depends on the nature of the problem, the characteristics of the data, and the desired outcomes. Regular evaluation and, if necessary, retraining are essential to maintain model accuracy as data patterns evolve over time.
The platform's flexibility allows users to train models on labeled data as well as unlabeled data, ensuring adaptability to unique business challenges. With a comprehensive training, testing, and validation framework, Using the Inferyx platform we can ensure the reliability and accuracy of deployed machine learning models.

Request the Download File
Choose
one
of the below options to download the patch file.
Option 1: Using
curl
To obtain the necessary download file for the patch, use the following
curl
command. This will authenticate and download the required tar file directly to your system.
curl https://dev.inferyx.com/download/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz
File Name:
inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz
OR
Option 2: Using
scp
Alternatively, you can securely copy (scp) the tar file from the remote server. Replace
<username>
,
<remote_host>
, and
/path/to/
with the appropriate values.
scp <username>@<remote_host>:/path/to/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz .
Note:
The dot (
.
) at the end of the command saves the file to your current working directory.
Once you have the tar file, extract it into the /install/framework/build/ directory:
tar -xvf /install/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5.tar.gz

cd /install/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5/deploy/bin
to the  Execute the
install_framework.sh
script with the required options do:
./install_framework.sh -h
./install_framework.sh -o all -a all -d framework -u inferyx -p 20Inferyx19
Run the create_all_db.sh Script

cd /app/framework/bin/
directory and run the
create_all_db.sh
script with the required parameters:
./create_all_db.sh -o common,inferyx -a all -s all -u inferyx -p inferyx -t mysql
License File
Request the license files from the Inferyx team and place them in the appropriate directory as specified by the documentation or instructions provided by the Inferyx team.
Run the deploy.sh script
Navigate to the
cd /install/framework/build/inferyx-platform-final-v5.4.4.5/deploy/bin
directory in the framework build and execute the
upgrade_framework.sh
script:
./upgrade_framework.sh -u inferyx -p 20Inferyx19 -b no -r no
The -r option is not mandatory. Use this option only if you need to patch the same version again.

URL
:

title: Install and Configure Mysql - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
First, navigate to the
/tmp
directory:
cd /tmp
Download the archive using
wget
by running the following command:
wget https://cdn.mysql.com/archives/mysql-8.0/mysql-server_8.0.20-2ubuntu20.04_amd64.deb-bundle.tar
OR
For Ubuntu 22 use this link other steps are same
sudo wget https://cdn.mysql.com/archives/mysql-8.0/mysql-server_8.0.33-1ubuntu22.04_amd64.deb-bundle.tar
tar -xvf mysql-server_8.0.33-1ubuntu22.04_amd64.deb-bundle.tar
Then, extract the archive you downloaded by running:
tar -xvf mysql-server_8.0.20-2ubuntu20.04_amd64.deb-bundle.tar
Now you can use
dpkg
to begin the install:
sudo dpkg -i  *.deb
During installation,  facing any error. Then, execute the quick fix commands:
sudo apt --fix-broken install
And then  you’ll be prompted with a configuration screen where you can set the username password.
You’ll configure the
my.cnf
file , under
/etc/mysql/my.cnf
. Update  the file for editing by running:
sudo nano /etc/mysql/my.cnf
Add the following lines end of files: /etc/mysql/my.cnf
[mysqld]
secure-file-priv = ""
local-infile=1
optimizer_search_depth=0
default_time_zone="+04:00"
[mysql]
local-infile=1
[client]
loose-local-infile=1
bind-address = 0.0.0.0
Now you can use
mysql
to begin the open MYSQL console:
mysql -u root -p
Now create a user
inferyx
and set password:
SET GLOBAL local_infile = 1;
CREATE USER 'inferyx'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED WITH mysql_native_password BY 'inferyx';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON * . * TO 'inferyx'@'localhost' with grant option;
CREATE USER 'inferyx'@'%' IDENTIFIED WITH mysql_native_password BY 'inferyx';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON * . * TO 'inferyx'@'%' with grant option;
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

title: Install and Configure JupyterHub 3.1.1 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
First, navigate to the
/opt
directory
cd /opt
Create a directory where
jupyterhub
will be installed
sudo mkdir -p /opt/jupyterhub/
cd /opt/jupyterhub/
Start by updating the system and installing necessary packages:
sudo apt-get update
Check the version of Python installed:
python3 --version
Install
Node.js
and
npm
:
sudo apt-get install npm nodejs
Install
pip
for Python 3:
sudo apt-get install python3-pip
Install
JupyterHub
using
pip
:
sudo python3 -m pip install jupyterhub
Install
Configurable HTTP Proxy
globally using npm:
sudo npm install -g configurable-http-proxy
Install
Jupyter Notebook
:
sudo python3 -m pip install notebook
Generate the default configuration file for JupyterHub:
sudo jupyterhub --generate-config
This command will create a
jupyterhub_config.py
file in the working directory.
Edit the
jupyterhub_config.py
file to configure the settings for your JupyterHub instance:
sudo nano jupyterhub_config.py
Add the following configuration settings:
c.JupyterHub.authenticator_class = 'jupyterhub.auth.PAMAuthenticator'
c.JupyterHub.base_url = '/jupyter'
c.JupyterHub.bind_url = 'http://0.0.0.0:8888'
c.JupyterHub.hub_bind_url = 'http://127.0.0.1:1010'
c.JupyterHub.ip = '0.0.0.0'
c.JupyterHub.port = 8888
c.Authenticator.admin_users = {'inferyx'}
c.Authenticator.allow_all = False
c.Authenticator.allowed_users = {'inferyx'}
c.Spawner.notebook_dir = '/home/{username}/'
c.Spawner.start_timeout = 120
c.LocalAuthenticator.create_system_users = True
c.ConfigurableHTTPProxy.api_url = 'http://127.0.0.1:8003'
Create a service file for JupyterHub
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/jupyterhub.service
Add the following configuration:
[Unit]
Description=JupyterHub
After=network.target
[Service]
User=root
Group=root
WorkingDirectory=/opt/jupyterhub
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/jupyterhub --config /opt/jupyterhub/jupyterhub_config.py
Restart=always
StandardOutput=append:/opt/jupyterhub/jupyterhub.log
StandardError=append:/opt/jupyterhub/jupyterhub.log
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Create the log file for JupyterHub to log its activities:
sudo touch /opt/jupyterhub/jupyterhub.log
Make sure the log file is writable:
sudo chmod 666 /opt/jupyterhub/jupyterhub.log
Enable the JupyterHub service to start on boot:
sudo systemctl enable jupyterhub
Start the JupyterHub service:
sudo systemctl start jupyterhub
Check the status of JupyterHub:
sudo systemctl status jupyterhub
Access JupyterHub
https://<your-server-ip>:8888/jupyter

content:
The algorithm is a set of well-defined instructions to solve a particular problem. The algorithm is part of the data science module.
How to design an Algorithm:
To write an algorithm, the following things are needed as pre-requisite:
The problem that is to be solved by this algorithm.
2. The problem's constraints must be considered while solving the problem.
3. The input to be taken to solve the problem.
4. The output to be expected when the problem is solved.
5. The solution to this problem, in the given constraints.
Use of algorithm in data science model:
In the context of data science models, algorithms serve as sophisticated mathematical models and mapping techniques. These computational tools are instrumental in unraveling hidden patterns within datasets. Machine learning, a subset of data science, employs a diverse set of algorithms capable of tasks such as pattern recognition, classification, and prediction. These algorithms learn and adapt by analyzing existing data, contributing to the model's ability to make informed decisions and generate valuable insights.
In the Inferyx platform, we integrate a variety of machine learning algorithms, encompassing both supervised and unsupervised learning approaches. Additionally, advanced analytics benefit from the incorporation of deep learning algorithms, enhancing the platform's capabilities to handle complex data patterns and relationships. This integration empowers users to perform in-depth analyses and gain actionable insights from their data, making Inferyx a robust solution for data-driven decision-making in diverse domains.

title: Feature - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
A feature  is a logical table of features that provides a single API for updating
feature values
, two different APIs - an online and an offline API - for reading feature values. In practice, features are stored in a dual database system with an online database storing the latest feature values in row-oriented table format  (e.g., OLTP databases or key-value stores) for fast feature retrieval during online inference, and the second offline database stores the historical feature values in a column-oriented store (e.g., data warehouse/lakehouse or object store). The offline API (to the offline store) is used to create training data and batch inference data. The data between the online and offline stores should be kept consistent by the feature store.
A feature  has an
entity_id
(or primary key), and optionally an
event_time
column (indicating when the features in that row were observed), and a
partition_key
(to layout the data in the offline store for faster query performance). Each row in the online store is uniquely identified by its
entity_id
(as the online store only stores the latest values for entities), but the offline store stores historical feature values for entities, so each row is uniquely identified by the (
entity_id
,
event_time
) pair. For example, for a credit card, the online store would only store the latest transaction feature data (with the credit card number as the
entity_id
), but the offline store would store all the historical transactions for that credit card - each transaction is uniquely identified by the credit card number and the timestamp for that transaction.
The other columns in a features are the features. You can select a particular feature (column) as the partition key for a feature  to determine how to layout the rows in the offline store such that you can efficiently query the data using queries with the
partition_key
. For example, if your partition key is the day and you have hundreds of days worth of data, with a day
partition_key
, you can query for a given day or a range of days, and only the data for those days will be read from disk (a push-down filter).
List - Feature list
Group - feature group

title: Install and Configure Neo4J - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Create Directory for Neo4j
cd /opt/
Download the Neo4j Enterprise tarball:
cd /opt/neo4j
sudo wget https://dist.neo4j.org/neo4j-enterprise-5.16.0-unix.tar.gz
Extract the downloaded tarball:
sudo tar -xvzf neo4j-enterprise-5.16.0-unix.tar.gz
Move the extracted files to the Neo4j directory:
#remove this
sudo mv neo4j-enterprise-5.16.0-unix /opt/neo4j
Ensure proper ownership:
sudo chown -R inferyx:inferyx /opt/neo4j
sudo chmod +x /opt/neo4j
Create the
/app/neo4j/config/
directory (new step)
sudo mkdir -p /app/neo4j/config/
Accept Neo4j License
Run the following command to accept the Neo4j evaluation license:
sudo /opt/neo4j/bin/neo4j-admin server license --accept-evaluation
Create a systemd service file to manage Neo4j:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/neo4j.service
Add the following content to the file:
[Unit]
Description=Neo4j Graph Database
After=network.target
[Service]
User=inferyx
Group=inferyx
ExecStart=/opt/neo4j/bin/neo4j start
ExecStop=/opt/neo4j/bin/neo4j stop
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=10
LimitNOFILE=60000
TimeoutSec=120
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Reload Systemd and Enable Neo4j Service
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable neo4j
Configure Neo4j
Create the directory and Configuration file for APOC configuration:
sudo mkdir -p /opt/neo4j/conf/
sudo touch /opt/neo4j/conf/apoc.conf
Edit the
apoc.conf
file:
sudo nano /opt/neo4j/conf/apoc.conf
Add the following content:
apoc.import.file.enabled=true
apoc.uuid.enabled=true
apoc.ttl.enabled=false
apoc.export.file.enabled=true
Update Neo4j Configuration
Edit the Neo4j configuration file:
sudo nano /opt/neo4j/conf/neo4j.conf
Update or add the following settings:
# Set the IP address Neo4j listens on
dbms.default_listen_address=0.0.0.0
# Advertise Neo4j on a specific IP
dbms.default_advertised_address=52.200.177.119
# Change the HTTP connector port (default is 7474)
dbms.connector.http.listen_address=:7474
# Change the Bolt connector port (default is 7687)
dbms.connector.bolt.listen_address=:7687
# Disable strict validation
server.config.strict_validation.enabled=false
# Allow specific procedures
dbms.security.procedures.allowlist=algo.*,apoc.*,gds.*
dbms.security.procedures.unrestricted=algo.*,apoc.*,gds.*
# Configure concurrency
gds.alpha.degree.writeConcurrency=24
gds.alpha.degree.readConcurrency=24
# Set the default listen address
server.default_listen_address=0.0.0.0
Update Environment Variables
Edit the Neo4j binary files to set
JAVA_HOME
(if needed):
sudo nano /opt/neo4j/bin/neo4j
sudo nano /opt/neo4j/bin/neo4j-admin
Add the following line (if not already present):
Scroll down to line 25 (Below REPO) and add the following line after it:
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64
Start the Neo4j service:
sudo systemctl start neo4j
Verify that Neo4j is running by checking the service status:
sudo systemctl status neo4j
Verify Neo4j Access
Open your web browser and navigate to the Neo4j web interface:
http://<server_ip>:7474
Replace
<server_ip>
with your server's IP address.

title: Install and Configure Camunda - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
First, navigate to the
/opt
directory
cd /opt
Create a directory where Camunda BPM will be installed
sudo mkdir /opt/camunda
Change the ownership of the directory to the desired user (in this case,
inferyx
)
sudo chown -R inferyx:inferyx /opt/camunda
sudo chmod +x  /opt/camunda
Navigate to the
/camunda
directory
cd /opt/camunda
Download the Camunda BPM Tomcat Distribution
To download the latest version of Camunda BPM Tomcat distribution, visit the official download page:
Download Latest Camunda BPM (Platform 7)
On the page, select Apache Tomcat as the distribution and download the latest version.
OR
Download the Camunda BPM Tomcat using the provided link
wget https://downloads.camunda.cloud/release/camunda-bpm/tomcat/7.21/camunda-bpm-tomcat-7.21.0.tar.gz
Extract the Camunda
Once the download is complete, extract the
.tar.gz
file
tar -xvzf camunda-bpm-tomcat-7.21.0.tar.gz
After extraction, the contents will be available in the
/opt/camunda
directory.
Change the Server Port
Open the
server.xml
file located in the
conf
directory of the extracted Camunda Tomcat installation:
cd /opt/camunda/server/apache-tomcat-9.0.75/conf/
Open
server.xml
for editing:
ls
sudo nano server.xml
Find the following line in the file
<Connector port="8080" protocol="HTTP/1.1" connectionTimeout="20000" redirectPort="8443" />
Change the port from
8080
to
9090
<Connector port="9090" protocol="HTTP/1.1" connectionTimeout="20000" redirectPort="8443" />
Change the Shutdown Port
In the
server.xml
file, find the
Server
element. It should look like this:
<Server port="8005" shutdown="SHUTDOWN">
Change the
port
attribute from
8005
to
9005
:
<Server port="9005" shutdown="SHUTDOWN">
Save the file (
Ctrl + X
, then
Y
, and press
Enter
).
Create a Systemd Service File for Camunda BPM
1. Create and Edit the Service File
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/camunda.service
Add the Following Configuration
[Unit]
Description=Camunda BPM Platform
After=network.target
[Service]
Type=forking
ExecStart=/opt/camunda/start-camunda.sh
ExecStop=/opt/camunda/shutdown-camunda.sh
User=inferyx
Group=inferyx
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5s
StartLimitIntervalSec=0
StartLimitBurst=0
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
Save the file and exit the editor (Ctrl + X, then Y, and press Enter).
Reload Systemd and Start the Camunda Service
Reload Systemd Daemon
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
Start the Camunda BPM Service
sudo service camunda start
Enable the Service to Start on Boot
ssudo service camunda enable
Check the Status of the Service
sudo service camunda status
Camunda BPM should now be running, and you can access the web interface at:
http://<your-server-ip>:9090/camunda
Camunda with MYSQL Configure
Log in to MySQL
mysql -u your-username -p
Enter your MySQL password when prompted .
Create the Database
CREATE DATABASE framework_camunda;
CREATE DATABASE
: This command initiates the creation of a new database.
framework_camunda
: This is the name of the database you want to create. You can replace this with any other valid database name.
Exit MySQL
EXIT;
Navigate to the
conf
directory:
cd /opt/camunda/server/apache-tomcat-9.0.75/conf
Open
nano bpm-platform.xml
for editing:
nano bpm-platform.xml
Locate the Line to Comment Out
Find the following line in the
<process-engine name="default">
section:
<datasource>java:jdbc/ProcessEngine</datasource>
Comment Out the Line
To comment out this line, add
<!--
at the beginning and
-->
at the end of the line. It should look like this
<!-- <datasource>java:jdbc/ProcessEngine</datasource> -->
This will prevent the line from being processed while keeping it in the file for future reference.
Locate the Section to Add Properties
Find the
<process-engine name="default">
section or the appropriate section where properties should be added.
Add Property Lines
Insert the following lines inside the
<properties>
element. Replace
database_name
,
username
, and
password
with your actual database name, username, and password
<property name="jdbcUrl">jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/database_name?characterEncoding=UTF-8&serverTimezone=UTC</property>
<property name="jdbcDriver">com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver</property>
<property name="jdbcUsername">username</property>
<property name="jdbcPassword">password</property>
Example Configuration
The modified section should look something like this
<process-engine name="default">
<job-acquisition>default</job-acquisition>
<configuration>org.camunda.bpm.engine.impl.cfg.StandaloneProcessEngineConfiguration</configuration>
<!-- <datasource>java:jdbc/ProcessEngine</datasource> -->
<properties>
<property name="jdbcUrl">jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/database_name?characterEncoding=UTF-8&serverTimezone=UTC</property>
<property name="jdbcDriver">com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver</property>
<property name="jdbcUsername">username</property>
<property name="jdbcPassword">password</property>
</properties>
</process-engine>
Save the file and exit the editor (Ctrl + X, then Y, and press Enter).
Download MySQL Connector
Download the MySQL Connector Java
.jar
file from the official Maven repository:
wget https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/mysql/mysql-connector-java/8.0.20/mysql-connector-java-8.0.20.jar
Copy the Connector to the Required Locations
After downloading, copy the
mysql-connector-java-8.0.20.jar
file to two specific locations where Camunda and Tomcat can access it:
Copy to Camunda's library folder:
sudo cp mysql-connector-java-8.0.20.jar /opt/camunda/lib/
Copy to Tomcat's library folder:
sudo cp mysql-connector-java-8.0.20.jar /opt/camunda/server/apache-tomcat-9.0.75/lib/
Start Camunda
sudo service start camunda
This command restarts the Camunda service, applying any configuration changes and ensuring that the service is running properly.
Resolve MySQL Table Not Found Issues
If you encounter errors related to missing MySQL tables (such as "Table not found"), you can execute the SQL scripts to create the necessary tables for the Camunda engine and identity management.
Run the following SQL scripts:
Create Camunda engine tables
mysql -u inferyx -ppassword databases < /opt/camunda/sql/create/mysql_engine_7.21.0.sql
Create Camunda identity tables:
mysql -u username -ppassword databases < /opt/camunda/sql/create/mysql_identity_7.21.0.sql
Restart Camunda
Once the MySQL tables have been created, restart Camunda to apply the changes:
sudo service restart camunda

First, navigate to the

#Unit contains the dependencies to be satisfied before the service is started.

# Key `User` specifies that the server will run under the mongodb user and

# `multi-user.target` means the server will be automatically started during boot.

WantedBy=multi-user.target

inferyx
ownership over the extracted installation by running:

port: 27017

#processManagement:

#replication:

folder under

inferyx
ownership over the extracted installation by running:

title: Install and Configure Kubeflow 1.26.0 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Prerequisites
Docker Installed
: Ensure Docker is installed and running.
docker --version
If not installed, follow Docker installation instructions.
Minikube Installed
: Ensure Minikube is installed.
minikube version
If Minikube is not installed:
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube
Add Docker Group
: Add your user to the Docker group.
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER
newgrp docker
Steps to Install and Configure Kubeflow Pipelines
1.
Start Minikube
minikube start --cpus=4 --memory=8192 --disk-size=30g
Verify Minikube status:
minikube status
2.
Install
kubectl
If
kubectl
is not installed:
sudo snap install kubectl --classic
Verify
kubectl
installation:
kubectl version --client
3.
Set the Kubeflow Pipelines Version
Set the environment variable for the Kubeflow Pipelines version:
export PIPELINE_VERSION=2.0.2
4.
Deploy Cluster-Scoped Resources
Apply the cluster-scoped resources:
kubectl apply -k "github.com/kubeflow/pipelines/manifests/kustomize/cluster-scoped-resources?ref=$PIPELINE_VERSION"
Wait for the resources to be established:
kubectl wait --for condition=established --timeout=60s crd/applications.app.k8s.io
5.
Deploy Environment-Specific Resources
Apply the environment-specific manifests for development:
kubectl apply -k "github.com/kubeflow/pipelines/manifests/kustomize/env/dev?ref=$PIPELINE_VERSION"
Verify pods are running:
kubectl get pods -A
6.
Update Port for the ML Pipeline UI Service
Patch the service to update the port:
kubectl patch svc ml-pipeline-ui -n kubeflow --type='json' -p '[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/ports/0/port", "value": 4041}]'
7.
Port-Forward the ML Pipeline UI
Forward the ML Pipeline UI service to a local port:
kubectl port-forward --address 0.0.0.0 -n kubeflow svc/ml-pipeline-ui 4042:4041
Access the UI at:
http://<your-minikube-ip>:4042

user:

/bin/bash
:

directory:

file:

inferyx

inferyx ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL

inferyx

title: Prediction - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Model prediction is the phase where a trained machine learning model is utilized to make predictions or infer outcomes based on new, unseen data. After undergoing the training process, during which the model learns patterns from labeled data, it becomes capable of generalizing its knowledge to make predictions on previously unseen instances. The steps involved in model prediction are as follows:
Input Data:
Provide new, unseen data to the trained machine learning model. This data should have the same features as the training data but lacks corresponding target labels.
Preprocessing:
Apply the same preprocessing steps used during training to ensure consistency and compatibility between the training and prediction phases.
Handle missing values, normalize or scale features, and perform any necessary transformations.
Model Inference:
Feed the preprocessed input data into the trained model.
The model utilizes the patterns learned during training to generate predictions or classifications for the new data.
Output:
Obtain the model's predictions as output.
The predictions can take various forms depending on the nature of the problem, such as class labels in classification tasks or numerical values in regression tasks.
Evaluation:
Evaluate the model's predictions using appropriate metrics to assess its accuracy and reliability.
This step helps confirm the model's generalization capabilities and its performance on real-world data.

content:
Introduction
A Vizpod represents a singular chart within the dashboard, allowing users to visualize specific data. Users have the flexibility to choose the Source Data, Chart Type, Key, and Values, as well as make cosmetic adjustments. This customization empowers users to effectively visualize and interpret data within the dashboard.
Create Vizpod

a) Fill in necessary fields > Click Submit

b) To create a Network Graph
A network graph is a visual representation of connections between different entities. It consists of nodes (representing entities) and edges (representing relationships or connections between entities). In simpler terms, it helps to illustrate how various things are related to each other in a structured way, making complex relationships easier to understand at a glance.
The Inferyx platform offers versatile visualization capabilities for network graphs. In this context, the source type is represented by Graphpods, which are knowledge graphs connected to the Neo4j database.
Creating a Network Graph involves utilizing a dedicated panel where users can input Cypher queries, allowing for dynamic and tailored exploration of the data. Cypher queries can be parameterized using placeholders, allowing their values to be replaced by filter values. This flexibility enhances the adaptability of the queries, enabling users to refine and tailor their exploration based on specific criteria or conditions.

title: Evaluation - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Model evaluation is a critical step in the machine learning workflow that assesses the performance and effectiveness of a trained model. The goal is to understand how well the model generalizes to new, unseen data and to identify any areas for improvement. The following steps are typically involved in model evaluation:
Test Data Set:
Separate a portion of the labeled data (not used during training) to create a test set. This set serves as a proxy for real-world, unseen data.
Prediction:
Use the trained model to make predictions on the test set. The model applies what it learned during training to new instances and generates output.
Evaluation Metrics:
Choose appropriate evaluation metrics based on the nature of the problem. Common metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC), among others.
Model evaluation is an ongoing process, especially as new data becomes available or the model is applied to different contexts. It ensures that machine learning models contribute meaningful and reliable insights to decision-making processes.

title: Install and Configure Genai (Optional) - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Prerequisites
Docker installed on the host machine.
Docker credentials provided by the Inferyx team (Docker Image).
1. Pull the Inferyx Docker Image
Retrieve the latest version of the Docker image from the registry.
docker pull inferyxhub/nlm-ingestor:latest
2. Run the Docker Container
Use the
docker run
command to start a container from the image.
This option maps the container directly to the host's network. Adjust volume mappings as needed.
docker run -d -p 5002:5001 --name nlm-ingestor inferyxhub/nlm-ingestor
3. OR Run the Docker Container (Automatically)
This option ensures that the container restarts automatically unless it was manually stopped. It's useful for running services that should always be available.
docker run -d -p 5002:5001 --name nlm-ingestor --restart unless-stopped inferyxhub/nlm-ingestor:latest

title: Install Python 3.7 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Install Python 3.7 – Default Method
First, install the prerequisite for adding custom PPAs.
sudo apt install software-properties-common -y
Second, add the
deadsnakes/ppa
to your APT package source list with the following command.
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:deadsnakes/ppa -y
Once the repository has been imported, run an
APT update
to fresh your package manager to reflect the new imported PPA.
sudo apt update
You can now install Python 3.7 by executing the following code:
sudo apt install python3.7 -y
To verify the installation and Python 3.7 build version, perform the following.
python3.7 --version
Install pip3 for Python 3
To install
pip3
(the package manager for Python 3), run the following command:
sudo apt install python3-pip
After installation, verify the
pip3
installation by checking its version:
pip3 --version
After installation upgrade pip
/usr/local/bin/python3.8 -m pip install --upgrade pip
Install Python 3.7 – Manual Method
Download the Python 3.7 archive.
wget https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.7.4/Python-3.7.4.tar.xz
Extract the Python archive. Remember to change the version number if you downloaded a newer one.
tar -xf Python-3.7.{version}.tar.xz
Optionally, move Python 3.7 to a proper destination such as the
/opt/
directory.
sudo mv Python3.7.{version} /opt/
Now install the dependencies required to install Python 3.7.
sudo apt install build-essential zlib1g-dev libncurses5-dev libgdbm-dev libnss3-dev libssl-dev libsqlite3-dev libreadline-dev libffi-dev curl libbz2-dev make -y
Navigate to the directory
cd /opt/Python3.7.{version}/
Now, run the
./configure –enable-optimizations
command.
./configure --enable-optimizations --enable-shared
install Python binaries as follows:
sudo make altinstall
Configure the dynamic linker run-time bindings:
sudo ldconfig /opt/Python3.7.{version}
Confirm that Python 3.7 is installed and the build version:
python3.7 --version
Install Python PIP 3.7
install PIP by downloading
get-pip.py
using the
wget command.
wget https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py
Next, install the file downloaded.
python3.7 get-pip.py
Once installed, it is a good idea to check for upgrades.
python3.7 -m pip install --upgrade pip
Now verify the PIP 3.7 version installed with the following command.
pip3.7 --version

sudo pip3.7 install alabaster==0.7.12

title: Generative AI - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Generative AI (GenAI) is a class of artificial intelligence models designed to create new content, such as text, images, music, or code, similar to human-produced content. These models learn from vast amounts of data and can generate novel outputs based on their identified patterns.
Large Language Models (LLMs) are a subset of GenAI focused on natural language processing. They are trained on enormous datasets of text, enabling them to understand and generate human language in a way that’s coherent and contextually relevant.
Here the platform is capable of the following :
Generating SQL Queries from Natural Language Input
Generating Cypher Queries from Natural Language Input
Retrieval-Augmented Document Generation for PDF-based Content

title: Install Python 3.8.10 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Step 1: Update System Packages
sudo apt update
Step 2: Install Dependencies
sudo apt install build-essential zlib1g-dev libncurses5-dev libgdbm-dev libnss3-dev libssl-dev libreadline-dev libffi-dev libsqlite3-dev wget curl llvm libbz2-dev
Step 3: Download Python 3.8.10
wget https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.8.10/Python-3.8.10.tgz
Step 4: Extract the Download
tar -xf Python-3.8.10.tgz
cd Python-3.8.10
Step 5: Configure the Build Environment
./configure --enable-optimizations
This command prepares the Python source code for installation and enables optimizations for better performance.
Step 6: Compile the Source Code
make -j$(nproc)
This command compiles the source code using the make utility. The -j$(nproc) flag tells make to use all available CPU cores for parallel compilation, speeding up the process.
Step 7: Install Python
sudo make altinstall
This command installs the compiled Python binary using sudo to gain the necessary permissions. The altinstall target prevents overwriting the default system Python.
Step 8: Verify Installation
python3.8 --version
Check the installed Python version to ensure Python 3.8.10 is installed correctly.
Install pip3 for Python 3

pip3
(the package manager for Python 3), run the following command:
sudo apt install python3-pip

installation by checking its version:
pip3 --version

/usr/local/bin/python3.8 -m pip install --upgrade pip

Install Python 3.8 (if not already installed):
Step 2: Install
virtualenv
Package (if not already installed)

This package is necessary for creating virtual environments.

Step 4: Set the Correct Permissions (if necessary)

source /opt/platformenv/bin/activate

title: Install and Configure Snowflake (Optional) - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Snowflake Account Creation and Sign-Up Guide
1.
Account Creations
Sign Up
:
Go to
https://signup.snowflake.com/
and fill out the registration form.
Login
:
Use the URL from the verification email to access the Snowflake Web Interface, then enter your email and password.
2. SnowSQL Installation and Connection Guide
1. Download and Install SnowSQL
a. Download SnowSQL
Use
wget
to download the SnowSQL installation script:
wget https://sfc-repo.snowflakecomputing.com/snowsql/bootstrap/1.2/linux_x86_64/snowsql-1.2.31-linux_x86_64.bash
b. Make the Installer Executable
Change the permission of the downloaded file to make it executable:
chmod +x snowsql-1.2.31-linux_x86_64.bash
c. Run the Installer
Execute the installer script with
sudo
:
sudo ./snowsql-1.2.31-linux_x86_64.bash
3. Specify Installation Directory
During the installation of SnowSQL, you will be prompted to specify the directory where SnowSQL components will be installed.
a. Installation Prompt
When prompted with the following message:
Specify the directory in which the SnowSQL components will be installed. [~/bin]
b. Enter Installation Directory
Type in your desired installation path (e.g.,
/opt/snowsql
) and press
Enter
:
/opt/snowsql
4. Update PATH Variable and Confirm
After specifying the installation directory, you will be prompted:
Do you want to add /opt/snowsql to PATH in /root/.profile? [y/N]
Type
y
and press
Enter
to confirm. This updates the
.profile
file, allowing you to run SnowSQL from any terminal.
Change Ownership and Permissions:
sudo chown inferyx:inferyx /opt/snowsql
sudo chmod +x /opt/snowsql
5. Open a New Terminal Session
After updating the PATH variable, open a new terminal session to apply the changes.
6. Test SnowSQL Connection
To verify your SnowSQL installation and connect to your Snowflake account, use the following command:
snowsql -a <account_name> -u <username>
Replace
<account_name>
with your Snowflake account name.
Replace
<username>
with your Snowflake login username.
7. Create SnowSQL Configuration File
Create Configuration Directory:
sudo mkdir ~/.snowsql
sudo chmod +x ~/.snowsql
sudo chown inferyx:inferyx ~/.snowsql
b.
Create Configuration File:
Edit the SnowSQL configuration file:
sudo nano ~/.snowsql/config
Add your connection information to the ~/.snowsql/config file:
[connections]
accountname = <account_name>
username = <login_name>
password = <password>
After saving the file, secure it by setting the proper permissions:
sudo chmod 600 ~/.snowsql/config
sudo chown inferyx:inferyx ~/.snowsql/config
8.
Create a Log File
To create a log file for SnowSQL:
sudo touch /snowsql_rt.log_bootstrap
sudo chown inferyx:inferyx /snowsql_rt.log_bootstrap
9. Set Environment Variables
Edit your
.bashrc
file to add SnowSQL-specific environment variables:
sudo nano ~/.bashrc
Add the following lines to the end of the file:
export PATH=$PATH:/opt/snowsql
After editing, reload the
.bashrc
file to apply the changes:
source ~/.bashrc
Secure SnowSQL Directory:
Ensure the SnowSQL directory has the correct ownership and permissions:
sudo chown -R inferyx:inferyx /home/inferyx/.snowsql
sudo chmod 755 /home/inferyx/.snowsql
10. Run SnowSQL
After completing the setup, you can start SnowSQL by simply typing:
snowsql
11. SnowSQL Interface
Once logged in, you will see the following interface:
SnowSQL * v1.2.32
Type SQL statements or !help
inferyx#COMPUTE_WH@(no database).(no schema)>

Download the Studio 3T Installer
First, download the Studio 3T installer for Linux from the

.
Extract the Tar File
Once the
tar.gz
file is downloaded, follow these steps to extract it:
Open the terminal.
Navigate to the folder where the
tar.gz
file is downloaded:
cd ~/Downloads
Extract the file using the following command:
tar -xvzf studio-3t-linux-x64.tar.gz
This will create a directory named
studio-3t-linux-x64
containing the extracted files.
Make the installation script executable (if it's not already):
chmod +x studio-3t-linux-x64.sh
Run the Installer
./studio-3t-linux-x64.sh
The script will begin by unpacking the required Java Runtime Environment (JRE) for Studio 3T to function correctly.
Follow the Installation Steps
The installer will open a graphical user interface (GUI) to guide you through the installation:
Choose the installation path
: You can either accept the default installation path or specify a custom directory.
Accept the End-User License Agreement (EULA):
Read and accept the terms of the EULA.
Proceed with installation
: Click the "Install" button to start the installation process.
Alternatively, a desktop shortcut may have been created during the installation process, which you can use to launch Studio 3T.
First-Time Setup
Upon launching Studio 3T for the first time, you’ll be prompted to connect to a MongoDB instance:
Enter the necessary connection details for your MongoDB server (e.g., hostname, port, authentication credentials).
After successfully connecting, you’ll be able to view your MongoDB databases and collections in the left sidebar of the Studio 3T interface.

title: DP Rule - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
A Data Profile Rule is a predefined criterion or set of criteria employed to assess and analyze specific characteristics of a dataset, typically aimed at gaining insights into its structure, quality, and content. These rules are systematically applied to the dataset to generate a comprehensive data profile, providing valuable information about various attributes, such as data distribution, uniqueness, completeness, and statistical summaries.
Data Profile Rules serve as a powerful tool in data management, enabling organizations to understand the nature and quality of their data. These rules can encompass a range of analyses, including but not limited to frequency distributions, pattern matching, uniqueness checks, and statistical computations. The results derived from the application of Data Profile Rules contribute to data governance efforts, aiding in the identification of data anomalies, outliers, and potential areas for improvement.
In essence, Data Profile Rules play a crucial role in the data profiling process, facilitating the exploration and understanding of dataset characteristics, which, in turn, supports data-driven decision-making and enhances overall data quality.

title: Install Platform Python(3.9) - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Prerequisites
Docker installed on the host machine.
Docker credentials provided by the Inferyx team (Docker Image).
1. Pull the Inferyx Docker Image
Retrieve the latest version of the Docker image from the registry.
docker pull inferyxhub/python39_pip
2. Run the Docker Container
Use the
docker run
command to start a container from the image.
This option maps the container directly to the host's network. Adjust volume mappings as needed.
docker run -it --network host --name python39_pip -v /app/framework:/app/framework -v /usr/lib/jvm:/usr/lib/jvm -v /var/log/inferyx:/var/log/inferyx -v /var/run/mysqld:/var/run/mysqld inferyxhub/python39_pip:latest
3.OR Run the Docker Container (Automatically)
This option ensures that the container restarts automatically unless it was manually stopped. It's useful for running services that should always be available.
docker run -it --network host --name python39_pip --restart unless-stopped -v /app/framework:/app/framework -v /usr/lib/jvm:/usr/lib/jvm -v /var/log/inferyx:/var/log/inferyx -v /var/run/mysqld:/var/run/mysqld inferyxhub/python39_pip:latest

title: Install or Upgrade Mongo 7.x - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
Before installing MongoDB 7.x, ensure that you take a dump of the existing MongoDB data, then stop any existing MongoDB  then proceed with the new installation.
Step : Take Backup of MongoDB Database
Before stopping MongoDB version 3.4.7, take a backup of the
framework
database.
Navigate to the backup directory:
cd /backup
Run the
mongodump
command to create a backup:
mongodump --host=localhost --port=27017 --db framework  --username=admin --password=20Admin19 --authenticationDatabase=admin
Step : Create a  Tar File
tar -czvf dump.tar.gz dump
After creating the tar file, install MongoDB 7.Once MongoDB 7 is installed, restore the MongoDB dump.The MongoDB restore steps are given below.
Stop Mongo 3.4.7 And Install Mongo 7.x
1.Verify MongoDB Service is running or not
sudo systemctl status mongodb
2. Stop MongoDB Service (if not already stopped)
sudo systemctl stop mongodb
3. Verify MongoDB Service is Stopped
sudo systemctl status mongodb
4. Backup MongoDB Directory
cd /opt/
sudo mv mongodb mongodb_bkp
5. Backup MongoDB Configuration File
cd /etc/
sudo mv mongod.conf mongod_bkp.conf
6. Backup MongoDB Service File
cd /etc/systemd/system/
sudo mv mongodb.service mongodb_bkp.service
7. Reload Systemd Daemon (to apply changes)
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
8.Check and backup storage data in
Open the MongoDB config file and check the
dbPath
.
cat /etc/mongod.conf
Look for the section:
storage:   dbPath: /var/lib/mongodb
If /var/lib/mongodb is the same path then take backup .
mv /var/lib/mongodb /var/lib/mongodb_bkp
Install Mongo 7.x
Update System Packages
sudo apt update
Remove any existing mongoDB GPG Key
To avoid conflicts, remove any outdated or existing GPG key for mongoDB:
sudo rm -f /usr/share/keyrings/mongodb-org-7.0.gpg
Import the MongoDB 7.0 GPG Key
curl -fsSL https://pgp.mongodb.com/server-7.0.asc | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/mongodb-org-7.0.gpg
Add the MongoDB Repository
echo "deb [ arch=amd64,arm64 signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/mongodb-org-7.0.gpg ] https://repo.mongodb.org/apt/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs)/mongodb-org/7.0 multiverse" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/mongodb-org-7.0.list
Update the Package Index
sudo apt update
Install MongoDB
sudo apt install -y mongodb-org
Set Correct Permissions
sudo chown -R mongodb:mongodb /var/lib/mongodb /var/log/mongodb
sudo chmod -R 755 /var/lib/mongodb /var/log/mongodb
Start the MongoDB Service
sudo systemctl start mongod
Check the MongoDB Service Status
sudo systemctl status mongod
Access MongoDB Shell
mongosh
Create Administrative Users
Switch to the
admin
Database
use admin
Create an Admin User
db.createUser({
user: "admin",
pwd: "20Admin19",
roles: ["root"]
});
Create an application user switch to the
framework
database and create the user:
db.createUser({
user: "inferyx",
pwd: "20Inferyx19",
roles: [
{ role: "read", db: "framework" },
{ role: "readWrite", db: "framework" },
{ role: "dbAdmin", db: "framework" }
]
});
Update MongoDB Bind IP If Needed
To allow remote connections to MongoDB:
Edit the MongoDB configuration file:
sudo nano /etc/mongod.conf
In the
net
section, change
bindIp
from
127.0.0.1
to
0.0.0.0
and enable security
net:
port: 27017
bindIp: 0.0.0.0
#security:
#authorization: enabled
To
security:
authorization: enabled
Save the file and restart MongoDB:
sudo systemctl restart mongod
Step : Restore the Mongo Backup
Log in to the target server and navigate to the backup directory:
cd /backup
Run the
mongodump
command to create a backup:
Extract the compressed backup file:
tar -xvf dump.tar.gz
Navigate to the extracted dump directory:
cd dump/
Restore the database using
mongorestore
:
mongorestore --host=localhost --port=27017 --db framework --username=admin --password=20Admin19 --authenticationDatabase=admin /backup/dump/framework

content:
Prerequisites
Docker installed on the host machine.
Docker credentials provided by the Inferyx team (Docker Image).
1. Pull the Inferyx Docker Image
Retrieve the latest version of the Docker image from the registry.
docker pull inferyxhub/python_pip:1.0.0
2. Run the Docker Container
Use the
docker run
command to start a container from the image.
This option maps the container directly to the host's network. Adjust volume mappings as needed.
docker run -d --restart unless-stopped --network host -it --name python_pip -v /app/framework/:/app/framework -v /usr/lib/jvm:/usr/lib/jvm -v /var/log/inferyx:/var/log/inferyx -v /var/run/mysqld:/var/run/mysqld inferyxhub/python_pip:1.0.0
3. Start and Stop docker container
docker start containerid
docker stop containerid

title: Install and Configure Tomcat 9.0 - Inferyx Platform Documentation - 5.x - Confluence
content:
First, navigate to the
/tmp
directory:
cd /tmp
Download the archive using
wget
by running the following command:
sudo wget https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat/tomcat-9/v9.0.83/bin/apache-tomcat-9.0.83.tar.gz
Then, extract the archive you downloaded by running:
sudo tar -xvf apache-tomcat-9.0.83.tar.gz
sudo mv apache-tomcat-9.0.83 /opt/tomcat
Since you have already created a user, you can now grant
inferyx
ownership over the extracted installation by running:
sudo chown -R inferyx:inferyx /opt/tomcat
sudo chmod -R u+x /opt/tomcat
You’ll store the
tomcat
service in a file named
tomcat
, under
/etc/init.d/
. Create the file for editing by running:
sudo nano /etc/init.d/tomcat
Add the following lines: /etc/init.d/tomcat
#!/bin/sh
export CATALINA_HOME="/opt/tomcat"
export JAVA_HOME="/usr/lib/jvm/java-1.8.0-openjdk-1.8.0.332.b09-2.el8_6.x86_64/jre"
RETVAL=$?
case $1 in
start)
if [ -f $CATALINA_HOME/bin/startup.sh ];
then
echo $"Starting Tomcat"
su -p -s /bin/sh inferyx $CATALINA_HOME/bin/startup.sh
fi
;;
stop)
if [ -f $CATALINA_HOME/bin/shutdown.sh ];
then
echo $"Stopping Tomcat"
su -p -s /bin/sh inferyx $CATALINA_HOME/bin/shutdown.sh
fi
;;
*)
echo $"Usage: $0 {start|stop}"
exit 1
;;
esac
exit $RETVAL
Modify the highlighted value of
JAVA_HOME
and
Tomcat
if it differs from the one you noted previously.
When you’re done, save and close the file.
You’ll store the
tomcat
service in a file named
tomcat.service
, under
/etc/systemd/system
. Create the file for editing by running:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/tomcat.service
Add the following lines: /etc/systemd/system/tomcat.service
[Unit]
Description=Apache Tomcat Web Application
After=network.target
[Service]
Type=forking
Environment=CATALINA_PID=/opt/tomcat/temp/tomcat.pid
Environment=CATALINA_HOME=/opt/tomcat
Environment=CATALINA_BASE=/opt/tomcat
ExecStart=/opt/tomcat/bin/startup.sh
ExecStop=/opt/tomcat/bin/shutdown.sh
User=inferyx
Group=inferyx
UMask=0007
RestartSec=10
Restart=always
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
When you’re done, save and close the file.
Reload the
systemd
daemon so that it becomes aware of the new service:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
Navigate to Tomcat's
bin
directory:
cd /opt/tomcat/bin
Create or edit the
setenv.sh
file:
nano setenv.sh
Add memory configurations to the
setenv.sh
file:
CATALINA_OPTS="-Xms4g -Xmx8g"
Environment=JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-8-openjdk-amd64
Modify the highlighted value of
JAVA_HOME
if it differs from the one you noted previously.
Save and exit
Ensure the
setenv.sh
file is executable:
chmod +x setenv.sh
After these steps, Tomcat will use the specified memory options when started.
You can then start the Tomcat service by typing:
sudo systemctl start tomcat
